main_summary.summary: Dr. Doris Derby, interviewed by Joe Mosnier on the campus of Georgia State University for the Civil Rights History Project, describes a path from a Williamsbridge, Bronx childhood through SNCC field organizing in Albany, Georgia (summer 1962) and Jackson, Mississippi (1963 onward). She traces family influences: maternal grandparents in Bangor, Maine (charter NAACP members); her great-aunt Jesse, a missionary in Monrovia, Liberia; and her aunt Julia Green, W.E.B. Du Bois's executive secretary later recruited to integrate the federal secretarial workforce in Washington. Derby studied dance at the Katherine Dunham school, used the Schomburg Library as a teenager, joined the NAACP at sixteen, and traveled to Nigeria on a 1960 NAACP scholarship before the country's independence. Through Hunter College's Christian Human Relations Club and AMSAC, she connected with the Northern Student Movement. After meeting Bob Zellner, Ruby Doris Smith, James Forman, and Charles Sherrod in Atlanta's SNCC office, she joined the Albany Movement, then accepted Bob Moses's invitation to a Mississippi adult-literacy pilot project tied to voter registration. In Jackson she co-founded the Free Southern Theatre with John O'Neal and Gilbert Moses, worked through COFO, taught Head Start outside Holly Springs and in Durant, helped build the Poor People's Corporation and Liberty House handcraft co-operatives, and documented the movement through Southern Media photography from 1968 onward.
key_themes: SNCC field organizing in Albany and Mississippi
Poor People's Corporation and Liberty House co-operatives
Pan-African cultural identification through Schomburg, AMSAC, and Nigeria travel
main_summary.historical_significance: Derby's testimony documents the cultural-arts arm of SNCC organizing in Mississippi from 1963 forward, an aspect of the movement less covered than direct-action campaigns. She provides first-person account of the Free Southern Theatre's founding rationale, the operational mechanics of COFO and the Poor People's Corporation handcraft co-operatives, and the recruitment, training, and facility logistics of early Head Start centers around Holly Springs and Durant. Her recollection of family precedents, a grandmother and uncle as charter NAACP members in 1920s Bangor, an aunt working for W.E.B. Du Bois, a great-aunt missionary in Liberia, establishes a multi-generational Black intellectual and activist lineage rooted outside the South.

chapters.title: A Bangor Grandmother
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier on the Georgia State campus for the project's inaugural year, Doris Derby opens with the family atmosphere that shaped her, the close gatherings of her mother's family of eleven children, and her maternal grandmother, born in Haiti and the Virgin Islands, who settled with a Virginia-born husband in Bangor, Maine.

chapters.title: Work Hard to Overcome
chapters.summary: Derby learned only later that her grandmother and eldest uncle were charter members of a 1920s Bangor NAACP chapter, and recalls her grandfather's beloved store, her Boy Scout-leader paternal grandfather, and the values both sides instilled, hard work, determination, and achievement for self, family, and community within a climate of racism, that led her to the Southern struggle.

chapters.title: Two Churches Every Sunday
chapters.summary: Derby describes growing up in the Williamsbridge section of the Bronx, an Episcopalian who also sang in the youth choir at her girlfriends' Baptist church down the block, in a varied neighborhood of Caribbean and Southern families, a Jewish delicatessen, an Italian liquor store, and Chinese and Black businesses.

chapters.title: Where Are Our Images
chapters.summary: From an early age Derby searched for positive Black images she could not find beyond Amos and Andy or Tarzan, and was drawn to the continent her great-aunt Jesse, a missionary in Liberia, wrote home about, beginning a lifelong mission to learn about and document African and African American culture.

chapters.title: The Schomburg and the Dunham Class
chapters.summary: Raised in a musical, dance-studying family, Derby won an audition for a Katherine Dunham dance scholarship that took her weekly to Harlem, where she also stopped at the Schomburg Library's collection of African and Caribbean heritage, and, inspired by her father's slide photography of family gatherings, resolved to create the images she could not find.

chapters.title: The Church Was the Hub
chapters.summary: Derby recalls meeting Katherine Dunham, joining the NAACP youth chapter that met at her church, the community hub for scouts, Bible school, and fashion shows, so that mass meetings later felt familiar, and the semi-rural edge of the Bronx where her family raised chickens and ducks, kept a vegetable garden, and made juice and jelly from a grape arbor.

chapters.title: He Left His Awards Behind
chapters.summary: Derby describes a politicized household where the children sat in on the conversations, and the dinner-table stories of discrimination, her father the only African American in his University of Pennsylvania civil engineering class, who left his gymnastics and fencing awards uncollected rather than enter by the service elevator, and of riding the rails north for work.

chapters.title: A Dentist Turned Skycap
chapters.summary: She recounts an uncle trained as a dentist at Tufts who, unable to find a partner or office, became a skycap at the airport with his intelligence intact, and her aunts who finished high school and played instruments, the family weighing their disparities and their children's goals at every holiday gathering.

chapters.title: Recruited to Washington
chapters.summary: Derby tells of her Aunt Julia, an executive secretary recruited as one of a handful of Black women to integrate the federal workforce in Washington, and how the roundtable family discussions, in a household that assumed college, shaped her own student conversations about Africa, the government, and where African Americans stood.

chapters.title: A Navajo Reservation
chapters.summary: Derby traces the traveling spirit she inherited, the church and Girl Scout trips of her youth and her interest in the Cherokee heritage her family claimed, to the summer she spent on a Navajo reservation in New Mexico as a budding cultural anthropologist who believed in living within a community to understand its life.

chapters.title: A Summer in Africa
chapters.summary: Derby recounts a friend's news that he was bound for Nigeria leading her to win an NAACP scholarship to Africa in the summer of 1960, before she ever went South, and her work with the African Scholarship Committee raising funds for the students brought over on the Kennedy airlift from newly independent countries.

chapters.title: What Could We Do to Support Them
chapters.summary: Derby describes the interracial Christian Human Relations Club at Hunter College, which sent a small group to talk with Southern student and community leaders about segregation, the sit-ins, and the freedom rides to learn how to support them, and the speakers it and the Northern Student Movement brought to campus, among them Ethel Kennedy and Malcolm X.

chapters.title: My Eyes Were Being Opened
chapters.summary: Derby reflects on how her widening view, Nigeria moving toward independence, stops in Paris and Rome, the economic inequality she saw among the Navajo and in Mexico, fit the struggles she had heard in her relatives' oral history, and how the American Society of African Culture drew her toward the cultural connections between Africa and African Americans.

chapters.title: There Is Not a Void
chapters.summary: Studying African dance, listening to African music, and bringing back African art to show at meetings, Derby made it her work to share the culture, later in Head Start programs and churches, against an American void she saw as purposely kept, and resolved to document the everyday and the artistic so others would have the visual images she had to search for.

chapters.title: Go to Atlanta First
chapters.summary: Derby recounts how a 1962 plan to pursue a master's in Mexico became a bus trip South to visit a sick, jailed Hunter colleague in Albany, Georgia, and how, because she had helped start a New York SNCC office, she was told she must first go to Atlanta to meet up with the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee.

chapters.title: An Integrated Car to Albany
chapters.summary: At the buzzing Atlanta SNCC office Derby met the key players, James Forman, Bob Zellner, Ruby Doris Smith, before riding in a then-dangerous integrated car with Forman and Zellner to Albany, where a family put her up and she was canvassing and reporting to the Albany Movement office by the next day, slipping at once into her lifelong community-helping mode.

chapters.title: All Hands on Deck
chapters.summary: Derby describes learning that her friend Peggy was jailed in Americus or Terrell County, the county people called Terrible Terrell, and a movement that needed all hands on deck as members were arrested with no help from the police; deploying her many skills, she let the master's plan go when Charles Sherrod asked her to return to New York to raise funds and goods for people thrown off their jobs.

chapters.title: Whatever We Need to Do
chapters.summary: Reflecting on the Albany summer of excitement, mass meetings, and nonviolence training, Derby credits the self-sufficiency and adaptability of her upbringing, a family that grew its own food, built and painted and sewed, and a father who taught her cabinetmaking and recycling, for letting her simply do what was needed rather than dwell on the danger.

chapters.title: A School Bus Full of Goods
chapters.summary: Back teaching in New York, Derby raised money and goods for the movement, partnering with a white woman who used a wheelchair, Rae Brandstein, to fill a school bus with canned food, clothing, and books, while expanding the network of people who could support the New York SNCC office she helped staff in the evenings.

chapters.title: New York Artists for SNCC
chapters.summary: Derby describes helping organize the March on Washington through Bayard Rustin's committee and co-founding New York Artists for SNCC, drawing on friendships with the visual artists, writers, and actors she had known since the Harlem Writers Guild and the American Society of African Culture, to donate work and stage benefit concerts for SNCC.

chapters.title: Bob Moses Calls
chapters.summary: Derby recounts inviting Bob Moses to speak at a successful New York fundraiser, after which he asked her to come to Mississippi to help build an adult literacy project tied to SNCC's voter registration, developing materials to meet the outrageous requirements placed on would-be voters; happy with her New York work and set on a master's, she at first said no.

chapters.title: The Least I Can Do
chapters.summary: Watching the news of the Birmingham violence that spring, the dogs and fire hoses turned on demonstrating children and adults, Derby resolved that the least she, a committed teacher, could do was join the literacy project, and called Bob Moses to say she would come to Mississippi after the March on Washington.

chapters.title: Full of Hope
chapters.summary: Derby remembers the March on Washington for the people of all walks of life and backgrounds gathered without the violence so many had predicted, the SNCC and community people she knew, and the sense that the day, full of hope, would make a difference.

chapters.title: Ten Dollars a Week
chapters.summary: Derby describes arriving in Jackson in the fall of 1963 as a SNCC field secretary paid ten dollars a week, housed with local families, working out of the buzzing COFO office on voter registration, demonstrations, and a mock governor's election, in an attitude that nothing was scripted in advance but it was time to do something, day by day, valuing the community people.

chapters.title: The Cracks in the Dam
chapters.summary: Unable to rely on the police or the FBI, Derby and her colleagues relied on themselves and the community, working to bring in the media and to insist the networks send Black crew members, noticing how white families' children drew more coverage, and looking always for the little cracks in a dam stacked against them, joined by some white Southerners who took real roles.

chapters.title: A Circle of Trust
chapters.summary: Derby explains that the divide between her Northern background and rural Black Mississippi was not so great for someone raised camping and handy in an extended-family way, and describes SNCC as a band of sisters and brothers in a circle of trust, humbled by local people whose bravery and deep spirituality, knowing full well what they risked, sustained the work.

chapters.title: The Women Were Strong
chapters.summary: Asked about her status as a woman, Derby says the question never faced her because she always did what she wanted, driven from within, and that with all hands on deck the women were strong, from organizers to the teenage girls who were willing to be arrested and jailed in rural counties with no facilities for them.

chapters.title: He Showed Me the Gun
chapters.summary: Derby recalls the constant danger of an integrated car with an out-of-state plate, and a gas-station moment in the early seventies when a man slid his jacket aside to show her a big gun, a single look that said everything; they left, because they had a goal and that was not the time, never knowing when such a situation would come.

chapters.title: We Need to Start a Theatre
chapters.summary: Derby describes how she, John O'Neal, and Gilbert Moses, all working on the literacy project and based at Tougaloo College, drew on her art and dance, their theater backgrounds, and her New York exposure to Black theater to conceive the Free Southern Theatre, with a sympathetic Tougaloo professor, Bill Hutchinson, drawn in.

chapters.title: Out to the Cotton Fields
chapters.summary: Derby explains that they imagined the theater as the vehicle and housing for the other arts, music, dance, and visual art, and called it free because they meant to take plays about voting and the movement out to the rural areas, performing in cotton fields, churches, and grocery stores wherever people could gather, an effort that lasted nineteen years.

chapters.title: Purlie Victorious
chapters.summary: Derby recalls the company's first production, Purlie Victorious, staged on campus in the spring of 1964, and the decision point as the year-long literacy project ran low on money: she went back to New York to try to renew the grant and continue the work with South Carolina migrants while the theater's base moved to Tulane under Richard Schechner, a move she disagreed with.

chapters.title: A Study of Achievement
chapters.summary: When the literacy grant did not come through, Derby joined a University of Michigan research project in the spring of 1965 studying Black students' achievement in predominantly white versus predominantly Black schools, conducted at Tougaloo, Talladega, and Tuskegee, before being asked to take up the Head Start work.

chapters.title: Teachers Afraid to Join
chapters.summary: Derby describes the very different push of Head Start, where whites opposed it and Black teachers with regular jobs were afraid to be associated with it, so teachers had to be recruited and trained from among college-attenders, high school graduates, and capable mothers; as a resource teacher near Holly Springs she made toys from recycled tires and wood, drawing on her father's craft.

chapters.title: We Made Our Own Books
chapters.summary: Lacking materials, Derby had the children react to pictures and turned their words into homemade books, brought in local musicians, and as a troubleshooting trainer reached the child development center in Durant, a beautiful building the Black community had built itself, where she was lead teacher for a documentary about the program called Chance for a Change.

chapters.title: Cooperatives and Liberty House
chapters.summary: Derby describes the Poor People's Corporation as an umbrella for economic development that promoted handcraft and farmer cooperatives across Mississippi, Louisiana, and Georgia, with Liberty House to market their goods; working through SNCC offices, she would go out to groups wanting to start a co-op and explain the minimum requirements of members, seed money, and a place.

chapters.title: The Black Rag Dolls
chapters.summary: Derby recounts recruiting Northern volunteer craftspeople and herself learning and teaching skills like candlemaking and leatherwork, and the black rag dolls of the Una sewing cooperative that became a big seller at a time such dolls were scarce; some of her work, including her SNCC blue-jean jumper dress, later traveled in the America I Am exhibit.

chapters.title: Train Us to Make Our Own
chapters.summary: Derby explains that Southern Media, begun in 1967 by two filmmakers, Bill Peltz and Geer Morton, to train young Black people to document their own lives and movement, came to the Poor People's Corporation needing someone with a stable base, and that she, always the experimenter, took it on, helping crew and make films across the state.

chapters.title: The Family Historian
chapters.summary: Splitting her time with Liberty House marketing travel across the country, Derby brought her family-historian instinct for keeping records to the camera, accumulating photographs and later, through the Black women's photography collective Cistography, finding the help to exhibit them, mindful that other movement photographers' images had been lost.

chapters.title: What the Photographs Show
chapters.summary: Derby describes what stands out when she looks at her photographs, women in all walks of life, the faces of children, and Black men doing constructive things against the stereotypes, alongside the flourishing cooperatives and the Tufts partnership with Mound Bayou on healthcare reform; she insists on showing the arts because creativity, more than mind alone, always finds a way, and has started a campus theater and filmmakers group.

chapters.title: A Black Womanist
chapters.summary: Derby closes on the black womanist philosophy she takes from Alice Walker, a Black woman working alongside her family and community, across all ages, to bring her people forward rather than apart from the men, and traces it to the open dinner-table discussions of issues, never hidden from the children, that she now carries in her love of exposing the young from an early age.

loc_video.caption: Doris Adelaide Derby oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Atlanta, Georgia, 2011 April 26
